chapter_context.summary: David attempts to bring the Ark of the Covenant to Jerusalem; Uzzah dies for touching it; David succeeds on the second attempt with great joy and dancing.

outline.text: First Attempt to Bring the Ark from Baale-judah on a New Cart
outline.explanation: This section focuses on First Attempt to Bring the Ark from Baale-judah on a New Cart (6:1-5), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Samuel.

outline.text: Uzzah Touches the Ark and Dies; Ark Left at House of Obed-edom
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Uzzah Touches the Ark and Dies; Ark Left at House of Obed-edom (6:6-11), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Samuel.

outline.text: Second Attempt Successful; David Dances with All His Might
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Second Attempt Successful; David Dances with All His Might (6:12-19), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Samuel.

outline.text: Michal Despises David's Worship; Michal's Barrenness
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Michal Despises David's Worship; Michal's Barrenness (6:20-23), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Samuel.

key_themes.title: The Davidic Covenant
key_themes.description: God's unconditional promise of an eternal dynasty, ultimately pointing forward to the Messiah.

key_themes.title: Sin and Consequences
key_themes.description: While God offers complete forgiveness for sincere repentance, the earthly consequences of sin remain.

devotional.question: How do the events of Chapter 6 demonstrate the grace of God in the midst of human failure?
What lessons about leadership and accountability can you apply to your own life from this chapter?

cultural_historical.title: Jerusalem as Capital
cultural_historical.text: By capturing Jerusalem, a neutral city on the border of Judah and Benjamin, David strategically united the fractured northern and southern tribes under a single capital.
